coredata.dc:title: CO2 activation on transition metal decorated graphene quantum dots: An insight from first principles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Kingkar
Mridha, Niranjan Kumar
Khan, Abdul Ashik
Baildya, Nabajyoti
Dutta, Tanmoy
Biswas, Kinkar
Ghosh, Narendra Nath
coredata.dc:description: Herein electrolytic reduction of CO2 on transition metal decorated graphene quantum dot (M@GQD) has been investigated by using first-principles calculations. Comparative study of geometrical properties and charge distribution of CO2 adsorbed M@GQD revealed that while GQD is unable to activate CO2, the transition metal decorated sites of M@GQD are available for CO2 adsorption, activation and dissociation. Charge distribution and molecular orbital analysis indicate that metal centre on GQD act as a Lewis base and the overall activation process is guided by the transfer of electron density from the corresponding metal centre to the π* orbital of CO2. The extent of CO2 activation is also analysed by the projected density of states (PDOS) which demonstrates that among the transition metal series Ti sites are available for CO2 activation and dissociation while activation does not occur involving Cu and Zn, due to their poor adsorption energy and lowest coupling of valence band state with the GQD. Overall transition metal decorated GQD opens up unlimited possibilities for designing new and highly selective and efficient catalysts for electrolytic CO2 reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
Graphene quantum dot
CO2 adsorption
Transition metals

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electronic structure study of isolated GQD and CO2 adsorbed GQD
Electronic structure analysis of transition metal decorated GQD
PDOS and molecular orbital analysis of M@GQD and CO2 adsorbed M@GQD
Transition state analysis of doped and un-doped hollow cages
Gibbs free energy analysis for activation process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We wish to acknowledge especially Gour Mahavidyalaya, Malda-732142, India for providing computational resources and Dr. Kumaresh Ghosh, department of Chemistry, University of Kalyani for supporting theoretical calculation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimised structures of transition metal decorated GQD.
Variation of binding energy with GQD decorated with transition metals.
Projected density of states (PDOS) of isolated GQD and transition metal decorated GQD.
Optimised structures of metal decorated GQD with adsorbed CO2.
Variation of adsorption energy of CO2 on M@GQDs
Variation of bond angle (degree) of adsorbed CO2 on M@GQDs
Mulliken charges for (a) On CO2 molecule after adsorption on GQD and M@GQDs; (b) On GQD after CO2 adsorption (red) and without CO2 adsorption (black).
Projected density of states (PDOS) of isolated GQD and M@GQDs.
Frontier Molecular Orbital distribution of the most stable configurations of CO2 adsorbed M@GQD
Molecular Electrostatic Potential maps for CO2@M@GQD
Activation energy of the CO2 adsorption process on CO2@M@GQD
Variation of Gibbs free energy and enthalpy during adsorption process of CO2 on M@GQD

head.ce:title: CO2 activation on transition metal decorated graphene quantum dots: An insight from first principles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein electrolytic reduction of CO2 on transition metal decorated graphene quantum dot (M@GQD) has been investigated by using first-principles calculations. Comparative study of geometrical properties and charge distribution of CO2 adsorbed M@GQD revealed that while GQD is unable to activate CO2, the transition metal decorated sites of M@GQD are available for CO2 adsorption, activation and dissociation. Charge distribution and molecular orbital analysis indicate that metal centre on GQD act as a Lewis base and the overall activation process is guided by the transfer of electron density from the corresponding metal centre to the π* orbital of CO2. The extent of CO2 activation is also analysed by the projected density of states (PDOS) which demonstrates that among the transition metal series Ti sites are available for CO2 activation and dissociation while activation does not occur involving Cu and Zn, due to their poor adsorption energy and lowest coupling of valence band state with the GQD. Overall transition metal decorated GQD opens up unlimited possibilities for designing new and highly selective and efficient catalysts for electrolytic CO2 reduction.

ce:section.ce:para: Reduction of CO2 to fuels like methane, methanol is an ample option for storing renewable energy and balancing global warming [1,2]. The converted fuel can be kept for long term fossil fuel alternative, chemicals for laboratory synthesis and can also be utilised, giving back CO2 as the main waste, terminating the overall loop. However stable linear geometry of CO2 has a strong C═O bond (750 kJ mol−1) making it challenging for electrochemical conversion [3]. Furthermore high activation barrier, multi-electron and proton transfer during the processes of different intermediates and products making the overall process highly complex [4,5]. During the recent decades several catalysts have been reported for the electrocatalytic transformation of CO2 but still remains as the biggest challenge. Metal oxides [6], zeolites [7] and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [8], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [9] and graphene have been reported as CO2 adsorber both experimentally and theoretically [10–12,47,48]. Graphene can be easily decorated and modified for catalytic reduction of CO2 to valuable energy resources [13]. Due to the unique characteristics like high thermal conductivity, zero bandgap semiconductor [14], large surface area [15], good tensile strength and high electron mobility, graphene offers various catalytic applications [16–18]. The electronic properties can be tuned by doping or adsorbed other materials or atoms with destroying the symmetry of the isolated graphene [19–21]. Due to the presence of mono-vacancy site of defected graphene, the binding affinity of Fe, Al and Ni nanoparticles are significantly enhanced thereby increasing the surface reactivity of the nanoparticle decorated graphene surface [22–24]. 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to their tunable bandgap arising due to their reduced size, offer outstanding physical and chemical properties among the carbon based family. Significant applications for graphene like electronics, catalysis, biomedicine, magnetism, energy storage ability [25], optical devices [26], bio-sensors [27] for detecting hazardous analytes including pesticides, antibiotics, heavy metals, and poisonous gases [28–31]. Previous studies showed that catalytic GQD-transition metal (Ni, Cu, Fe, Co, Ag) nanocomposites are highly effective for hydrogen evaluation [32,33].

Most recent, graphene decoration with metal have an most interesting to the researcher for its high stability and long lasting capacity in the harsh reaction conditions [34,35]. Johll et al. reported the adsorption of Fe, Co and Ni on graphene with binding energies in the range 0.2–1.4 eV [36]. At the same time, it has been reported that transition metal surface is sensitive towards CO2 activation and different structure have different surface activities [37]. GQD is an excellent material for selective transition metal detection. Recently some experimental studies showed that CO2 can be converted into fuel on transition-metal single-atom loaded GQD where metal atomic site exhibits extraordinary catalytic activities [38,39]. These studies also illustrated that the electrochemical catalytic activities of CO2 reduction on single-atom catalyst on GQD enhances with increase in nickel loading [38]. Hence transition metal decorated GQDs should offer excellent possibilities of catalytic electrochemical reduction of CO2 to fuel.

In this study, we have made a comprehensive analysis of the feasibility of CO2 activation by employing density functional theory (DFT) with decorating the 3d transition metals (Sc to Zn) on GQD in an atomistic level. We have analysed different parameters viz. bandgap, partial density of state (PDOS), charge distribution and adsorption properties with 3d-transition elements during CO2 adsorption and activation.

All density functional calculations of the bare GQD, M@GQD and CO2@M@GQD (M = Sc, Ti, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu and Zn) were performed with B3LYP hybrid functional [40] using Gaussian 09 programme [41]. For non-metals (C,H,O) localized 6-31G(d) basis set was used, while for metals effective core potential LANL2DZ basis set [42] was employed. Adsorption energies (Eads) of CO2@M@GQD were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
E
a d s
=
E
C
O 2
@ M @ G Q D
−
E
M @ G Q D
−
E
C
O 2
Where,
E
CO 2
@ M @ GQD
is the total energy of the adsorbed CO2 on the M@GQD, and
E
M @ GQD
and
E
CO 2
are the total energies of the M@GQD and free CO2 respectively. By using Frozen String method (implemented in Q-Chem 5.2 software package [43]), transition states are analysed after the activation of CO2 on the M@GQD surface. To determine the transition state, all spacing parameter (20 nodes, 6 perpendicular gradients per node) are considered. For initiating the FSM calculations the equivalent input files were eliminated to ensure not to pay the cost for computation on replicated structures. Different electro-chemical parameters like adsorption energy, HOMO-LUMO were evaluated using the same level of theory. The density of states (DOS), molecular electrostatic potential surface (MEP) and frontier molecular orbital were analysed. To understand the amount of electron transfer from the GQD/M@GQD to CO2 Mulliken charge, dipole moment were also computed. Gausssum program was used to perform for plotting projected density of state and density of state for each individual GQD, M@GQD and CO2 adsorbed systems [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic structure study of isolated GQD and CO2 adsorbed GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 illustrates the optimised structures of GQD and CO2 adsorbed GQD respectively. Since there is no alternation of O–C–O bond angle and C–O bond length of CO2 after adsorption on GQD, CO2 activation does not occur on GQD in pure form. Although the slight negative Eads (−0.017 eV) indicates that CO2 adsorption on GQD is a thermodynamically favourable process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic structure analysis of transition metal decorated GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Since pure GQD is not suitable for the activation of CO2, we have decorated the GQD with single 3d transition metals (from Sc to Zn) to form nano-composites. Optimised geometries with stable configurations of transition metals on GQD are shown in Fig. 2 . Different metals have different interactions with GQD. The distance of different metals from GQD is in the range of 1.56–4.73 Å, while the binding energies fall in the range between −0.75 and 1.16 eV, as shown in Figs. 2 and 3 respectively which is concordant with the results of recent studies [45]. The highest adsorption energy is achieved by Cr while the lowest adsorption energy is experienced by Zn on GQD. The ionic character in M@GQD arises due to charge transfer from transition metals to the GQD which is demonstrated by the Mulliken charge analysis as presented in Fig. 8b. The interaction of Cu and Zn is minimal as only the sp-orbitals of adatom are involved in the hybridization with the GQD sp-band which is in agreement with substantial weaker bonding with Cu and Zn in the same transition metal series.
The adatom adsorption strength on GQD follows the order Cr > Mn > Ni > Ti > Fe > Co > Cu > Zn, which is consistent with the GQD-metal bond length from 1.562 to 4.727 Å.

To understand the electronic structure of nano-composites the M@GQD we have carried out projected density of states (PDOS) analysis. Fig. 4 represents the PDOS of bare GQD and transition metals decorated GQD. From the PDOS analysis, it is clear that valence band of the nano-composite is arising from the metal while the conduction band has a contribution of both metal and GQD. In case of Cu and Zn, the valence and conduction band has no contribution from the metal end which is attributed by the weak interaction of these metals with GQD. The strong interaction with the transition metals (except Cu and Zn) is seen from the strong coupling of valence and conduction band states of GQD and transition metals.

Analysing the electronic structure of M@GQD, we have investigated the adsorption behaviour of CO2 on the corresponding M@GQD nano-composites. Fig. 5 represents the optimised structures of CO2 adsorbed metal decorated GQDs. Fig. 3 illustrates the distances of the different metal sites to the adsorbed CO2. Lowest M-CO2 distance is achieved with Fe@GQD (1.84 Å) while Zn@GQD realizes the highest distance (4.22 Å). Compared to the isolated GQD, the Eads of CO2 on all the M@GQDs are much higher, indicating the adsorption process is highly feasible involving M@GQD nano-composites. This is consistent with the previous report by Promthong et al. that the metal decorated graphene provides stronger interaction with CO2 surface than the perfect pristine graphene [46]. The CO2 adsorption energies on transition metal decorated GQD are shown in Fig. 6 . Much negative values of Eads for Sc and Ti decorated GQD favours CO2 adsorption while for Cu and Zn least adsorption energies are noticed. On the other hand, Cr@GQD, Mn@GQD, Fe@GQD, Co@GQD and Ni@GQD have moderate interaction with CO2 as seen from the geometric parameter of the corresponding optimised structures.

Fig. 7 showed that O–C–O bond angle deviates from the linearity in all M@GQD except Cu and Zn cases. Maximum deviation of O–C–O bond angle is observed with Sc@GQD while with Ti@GQDCO2 dissociation of CO2 occurs. Furthermore, during the process of adsorption, the stretching of CO bond length are also noticed.
Except for Cu and Zn, in adsorbed CO2, lengthening of the C–O bond length indicates that significant amount of electron density is transferred from the metal site to the π*-orbital of CO2.

From the analysis of bond angle, bond length, Eads and M-CO2 distances it is clear that the transition metal decorated GQD (except Cu and Zn) behave as an efficient nano-catalyst for CO2 activation. Here we also note that both Cu and Zn have stable d10 electronic configuration and much reluctant to donate electron density to CO2 and which explains the unactivation process involving them.

In order to understand the mechanism of CO2 activation, Mulliken charge analysis of CO2@M@GQD were performed. The amount of electron transfer between CO2 and GQD species are illustrated in Fig. 8 a which showed that CO2 gained electron density due to transfer of electron from M@GQD moiety. Highest Mulliken charge is transferred from Sc@GQD (−0.609e) to CO2 and lowest from Cu@GQD (0.16e) while highest charge was present in Ti@GQD (−0.875e) and lowest on Cu@GQD (0.046e). Furthermore, we have also analysed the sum of the Mulliken charges on GQD before (black line) and after (red line) CO2 adsorption as shown in Fig. 8b. Systematic analysis revealed that after CO2 adsorption Mulliken charge on M@GQD reduces significantly suggesting that both metal centre and GQD combinedly participated in CO2 activation process. The reverse trend is followed with Cu/Zn@GQD system and therefore CO2 adsorption does not occur involving them. It is clear that compared to pristine GQD, M@GQD (except Cu and Zn) act as a stronger Lewis base which greatly enhances the adsorption and activation capacity of CO2 (see Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PDOS and molecular orbital analysis of M@GQD and CO2 adsorbed M@GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the extent of CO2 activation we have performed PDOS for CO2 adsorbed M@GQD composite. In case of free CO2 the bonding and anti-bonding π* MOs are degenerate in nature while in case of activated CO2 the degeneracy of the corresponding state are destroyed due to the incorporation of electron density from the metal decorated site (see Fig. 9). The amount of electron transferred from metal to CO2 is different which is confirmed from the different splitting of those states. In case of Cu and Zn, the degeneracy of corresponding state is retained which suggests CO2 is weakly bonded involving these site. Furthermore, PDOS of the adsorbed and free M@GQD revealed that CO2 adsorption significantly lifted the valence band states confirming substantial electron transfer from metal to CO2.

HOMO-LUMO charge density of before and after adsorption on M@GQD nano-composites are shown in Fig. 10 . For all the system HOMO is localized mostly on metal adsorbed CO2 while the LUMO is concentrated on GQD further confirming that after adsorption metal bound CO2 moiety gained electron density from GQD.

To further analyze the molecular interaction between CO2 and GQD molecular electrostatic potential map is plotted as shown in Fig. 11 . All electro static potential maps indicate strong interaction between the metal site and CO2 (red region) except Cu and Zn concluding very weak interaction between these sites with CO2 (blue region).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition state analysis of doped and un-doped hollow cages
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 illustrates the activation energy changes of the CO2 molecule adsorption on the different M@GQD surfaces. The activation energy ranges between 0.043 and 1.11 eV. Highest value of activation energy is achieved by CO2@Co@GQD system while lowest is experienced CO2@Ti@GQD system. Here we note that for CO2@Ti@GQD system the high adsorption enegy and low activation barrier strongly suggests the facile activation of CO2 molecule at ambient temperature involving this nano composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gibbs free energy analysis for activation process
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the feasibility of CO2 adsorption on metal decorated GQD, we have calculated different thermodynamic parameters. Gibbs free energy change after CO2 adsorption is illustrated in Fig. 13 which clearly demonstrates that CO2 adsorption is thermodynamically favourable. Among the transition metal series, CO2@Ti@GQD achieves highest Gibbs free energy while Cu and Zn experienced lowest one. We also note that the dissociation of CO2 occurring Ti@GQD surface is a thermodynamically demanding process. The enthalpy change is shown in Fig. 13b. The negative enthalpy values for all the systems indicate that the overall adsorption is an enthalpy driven process.

In the present work we have made a comprehensive analysis of CO2 adsorption, activation and dissociation over a series of transition metal decorated GQDs. The results clearly indicates that while no adsorption and activation is seen with free GQD, transition metal centres (except Cu and Zn) of M@GQD offer suitable sites for CO2 adsorption and activation. Along the transition metal series highest adsorption energy is found with Ti where the dissociation of CO2 occurs, on the other hand least adsorption energy is realised with Cu and Zn. Analysis of O–C–O bond angle and Mulliken charge revealed that a substantial amount of electron density is transferred from M@GQD to vacant π* orbital of CO2. High negative values of enthalpy as well as Gibbs free energy revealed that the overall adsorption process is thermodynamically favourable. Furthermore, PDOS analysis confirmed that Zn and Cu decorated GQD can not activate CO2 due to the lowest coupling of valence band state with the GQD and minimal interaction takes place. Electronic charge distribution of HOMO and LUMO of CO2@M@GQD suggests that M@GQD act as a Lewis base for CO2 adsorption. Overall except Cu and Zn, M@GQD (M = Sc, Ti, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni) is suitable nanocatalyst for CO2 activation and open up a new avenue for next-generation catalyst for electrolytic reduction of CO2 to fuel.
The authors acknowledge no financial support from any funding resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The Authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to acknowledge especially Gour Mahavidyalaya, Malda-732142, India for providing computational resources and Dr. Kumaresh Ghosh, department of Chemistry, University of Kalyani for supporting theoretical calculation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular simulation studies of CO2 adsorption by carbon model compounds for carbon capture and sequestration applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, J. Wilcox, Molecular simulation studies of CO2 adsorption by carbon model compounds for carbon capture and sequestration applications, Environmental science & technology, 47 (2013) 95-101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular simulation of CO2 adsorption in micro-and mesoporous carbons with surface heterogeneity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, J. Wilcox, Molecular simulation of CO2 adsorption in micro-and mesoporous carbons with surface heterogeneity, International Journal of Coal Geology, 104 (2012) 83-95.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Li, B. Peng, T. Peng, Recent advances in heterogeneous photocatalytic CO2 conversion to solar fuels, ACS Catalysis, 6 (2016) 7485-7527.
